events.event_label: Notabilis overexpression enhances thermotolerance, hypocotyl length and seedling survival
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s016: Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h). PMC7540533.p029.s017: We then measured the relative abundance of HS marker genes in the silenced Notabilis plants; the level of all the genes was reduced several folds upon HS in comparison with control (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s018: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s019: Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance.

events.event_label: Acylsugar acyltransferase and Notabilis confer HS tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p003.s003: Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation. PMC7540533.p003.s004: The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses. PMC7540533.p003.s005: Functional characterization of three antagonistic genes by overexpression and silencing established Solyc09g014280 (Acylsugar acyltransferase) and Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis) that are up‐regulated in tolerant cultivar, as positive regulators of HS tolerance and Solyc03g020030 (Pin‐II proteinase inhibitor), that are down‐regulated in CLN1621L, as negative regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p003.s006: Transcriptional assessment of promoters of these genes by SNPs in stress‐responsive cis‐elements and promoter swapping experiments in opposite cultivar background showed inherent cultivar‐specific orchestration of transcription factors in regulating transcription. PMC7540533.p003.s007: Moreover, overexpression of three ethylene response transcription factors (ERF.C1/F4/F5) also improved HS tolerance in tomato.

events.event_label: HSP21 protects PSII and contributes to fruit ripening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s005: Tomato HSFA2 is highly up‐regulated during HS that in turn forms a hetero‐oligomeric transcriptional complex with HSFA1. PMC7540533.p006.s006: In addition, HSP17.4‐CII acts as a co‐repressor of HSFA2 (Port et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s007: Overexpression of Arabidopsis receptor‐like kinase ERECTA (ER) leads to enhanced HS tolerance in Arabidopsis, rice and tomato with increased biomass (Shen et al.,).

events.event_label: HS upregulates HSFA2 which interacts with HSFA1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s005: Tomato HSFA2 is highly up‐regulated during HS that in turn forms a hetero‐oligomeric transcriptional complex with HSFA1. PMC7540533.p006.s006: In addition, HSP17.4‐CII acts as a co‐repressor of HSFA2 (Port et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s007: Overexpression of Arabidopsis receptor‐like kinase ERECTA (ER) leads to enhanced HS tolerance in Arabidopsis, rice and tomato with increased biomass (Shen et al.,).

events.event_label: Proteinase inhibitors and HXXXD-type acyltransferases contribute to plant defence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s009: Solyc01g065530 (protein COBRA), Solyc01g081250 (glutathione s‐transferase), Solyc01g087020 (transmembrane protein), Solyc01g095140 (late embryogenesis abundant protein), Solyc01g107780 (glycosyltransferase), Solyc03g116890 (WRKY transcription factor 39), Soly05g052670, Solyc05g052680 and Solyc09g014280 (all three HXXXD‐type acyltransferase family proteins), Solyc06g049070 (nucleotide/sugar transporter family protein), Solyc07g008103 (blue copper protein), Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis), Solyc07g064410 (Kua‐ubiquitin conjugating enzyme hybrid), Solyc09g075920 (serine/threonine‐protein kinase), Solyc09g092500 (glycosyltransferase) and Solyc10g081570 (Marmande), were up‐regulated in CLN but down‐regulated in CA4 upon HS. PMC7540533.p014.s010: Gene ontology (GO) enrichment of these antagonistic genes highlighted the significant enrichment of negative regulation of endopeptidase, peptidase and proteolysis, response to water, pollination and transferase activity (Figure 2c). PMC7540533.p014.s011: Among these, five (the two proteinase inhibitors and the three HXXXD‐type acyltransferases) have roles in plant defence. PMC7540533.p014.s012: The qRT‐PCR validation of 10 genes selected from the above clusters confirmed the opposite HS regulation in CA4 and CLN (Figure 2d).

events.event_label: HS up‑regulates HSF family members HSF and HSFA2
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

events.event_label: HS up‑regulates HSFA6a and down‑regulates HSFA4b
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s006: The Arabidopsis hsfb1‐hsfb2b double knockout mutant demonstrates higher acquired thermotolerance (Ikeda et al.,; Kumar et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s007: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s008: AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s009: Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s010: Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ).

events.event_label: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 are localized in glandular trichomes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s005: While Solyc09g014280 is induced upon potato cyst nematode infection in tomato roots (Swiecicka et al.,), Solyc05g052670 is up‐regulated in tomato in response to tomato yellow leaf curl virus infection (Chen et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s006: Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p026.s007: Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a).

events.event_label: NCED6 and NCED9 affect thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst.

events.event_label: PI knock‑down decreases transpiration rate and stomatal conductance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).

events.event_label: bZIP28, HSFA2 and ROS participate in heat response pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s014: Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s015: The significance of post‐translational regulation in HS response is also well documented in plant HSR (Agarwal et al.,; Hahn et al.,; Miller et al.,; Mizoi et al.,; Sato et al.,; Vainonen et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s016: Evidences also suggest orchestration of multi‐layered regulatory systems comprising not only TFs but also epigenetic regulators and small RNAs as important HSR players.

events.event_label: HSPs, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation involved in HS response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s001: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world. PMC7540533.p006.s002: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,).

events.event_label: TaNCED overexpression improves drought tolerance and reduces stomatal conductance and transpiration
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s014: There was a significant reduction in the photosynthetic rate and water‐use efficiency along with a significant rise in stomatal conductance and enhanced transpiration rate in silenced plants in contrast to vector control plants (Figure 4c–f). PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s016: Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h).

events.event_label: NAC019 regulates HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

events.event_label: ZAT12, NAC019, bZIP28, and PIF4 contribute to thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation. PMC7540533.p020.s017: Using these criteria, members belonging to TF families B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix were majorly induced in response to HS, while ARF, Dof, MADS, TCP and YABBY were primarily repressed in response to HS (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s018: Thermotolerance in transgenic systems has been achieved by modulating the levels of several TFs like ZAT12 (Shah et al.,), NAC019 (Guan et al.,), bZIP28 (Srivastava et al.,) and PIF4 (a bHLH transcription factor) in Arabidopsis (Gangappa et al.,; Hwang et al.,).

events.event_label: ASAT catalyzes sucrose modification using acyl CoA and produces Acylsugars
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

events.event_label: ASAT silencing under heat stress reduces net photosynthesis and WUEi, increases stomatal conductance and transpiration, and impairs thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

events.event_label: HS induces B3, C3H, HSF, NAC, Trihelix and represses ARF, Dof, MADS, TCP, YABBY
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation. PMC7540533.p020.s017: Using these criteria, members belonging to TF families B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix were majorly induced in response to HS, while ARF, Dof, MADS, TCP and YABBY were primarily repressed in response to HS (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s018: Thermotolerance in transgenic systems has been achieved by modulating the levels of several TFs like ZAT12 (Shah et al.,), NAC019 (Guan et al.,), bZIP28 (Srivastava et al.,) and PIF4 (a bHLH transcription factor) in Arabidopsis (Gangappa et al.,; Hwang et al.,).

events.event_label: NGATHA1 activates NCED3 leading to ABA biosynthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s005: In lettuce, its homolog (NCED4) is the causal gene in the Htg6.1 QTL, associated with thermo‐inhibition of seed germination (Huo et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a).

events.event_label: HSFA2a and HSFA7a contribute to thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s018: Moreover, when we checked the expression of these HSR genes in response to ABA, they were all up‐regulated, suggesting Notabilis as an upstream regulator of these HS marker genes (Figure 4i). PMC7540533.p029.s019: Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s020: Furthermore, we asked whether the opposite expression of Notabilis in CLN‐CA4 cultivars is because of variation in promoter sequences and/or an inherent difference in TF pool of both the cultivars. PMC7540533.p029.s021: Sequence information of promoters for both the varieties highlighted no SNPs; thus, a regulatory influence of the cis‐sites in the contrasting expression was ruled out. PMC7540533.p029.s022: We then checked the expression of GUS gene hooked to Notabilis promoter in CLN and CA4 background.

events.event_label: Notabilis silencing increases ROS and HS‑induced cell death
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. PMC7540533.p029.s011: However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b). events.event_label: ASAT gene modulates thermotolerance and physiological traits
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s006: Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p026.s007: Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p026.s008: Heat stress results in a higher than optimal concentration of ROS that not only affect plant’s ability to photosynthesize but also cause cell death. PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h). PMC7540533.p026.s021: Many of these TFs showed differential expression pattern between the two cultivars (Table S4 and Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p026.s022: Our data demonstrated a novel role of ASAT as a positive regulator of tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s023: The exact mode of action of this enzyme under HS needs further attention.

PMC7540533.p029.s002: Notabilis regulates the rate‐limiting step in the ABA biosynthesis pathway and has important role in water stress tolerance as evidenced by several reports (Burbidge et al.,; He et al.,; Luchi et al.,; Qin and Zeevaart,; Wan and Li,). PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s005: In lettuce, its homolog (NCED4) is the causal gene in the Htg6.1 QTL, associated with thermo‐inhibition of seed germination (Huo et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst.

events.event_label: HS and ABA regulate expression of heat‑stress transcription factors
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated. PMC7540533.p022.s006: The Arabidopsis hsfb1‐hsfb2b double knockout mutant demonstrates higher acquired thermotolerance (Ikeda et al.,; Kumar et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s007: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s008: AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s009: Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s010: Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ). PMC7540533.p022.s011: Out of 147 ERFs reported in tomato, 30 members followed differential HS response in CLN and CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. PMC7540533.p022.s013: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS.

events.event_label: HS modulates transcription factor expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s003: Out of these, 402 TFs belonging to 46 families were found to be heat‐responsive in at least one cultivar. PMC7540533.p020.s004: In CLN and CA4, 314 (152 down/162 up) and 285 (152 down/133 up) TFs were significantly differentially regulated, respectively (Figure 3a, Table S4). PMC7540533.p020.s005: Similar HS response was evident for 201 (100 down/101 up) TFs in both cultivars (Figure 3a). PMC7540533.p020.s006: Thirty‐seven TFs (29 and 16 in CLN and CA4, respectively) exhibited very strong heat induction (FC > 10). PMC7540533.p020.s007: Thirty TFs followed conserved high (FC > 5) up‐regulation in both the cultivars, while 26 followed high (FC < −5) down‐regulation in both the cultivars. PMC7540533.p020.s009: Of these, 3 TFs, viz. PMC7540533.p020.s010: HSFA2 (Solyc08g062960), and dehydration‐responsive element‐binding (DREB) TF (Solyc05g052410) were more than 100‐fold up‐regulated in both cultivars. PMC7540533.p020.s011: The plot of above 30 TFs clearly revealed that the heat‐tolerant CLN showed higher magnitude of HS‐mediated induction in majority of the TFs and common key TF pool was activated upon HS in both cultivars (Figure 3b). PMC7540533.p020.s012: Moreover, 26 TFs showed HS‐mediated strong repression (FC ≤ 5) in both cultivars (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s013: While Solyc08g008280 (WRKY‐53) was the top repressed candidate in CLN, Solyc10g005080 (late elongated hypocotyl) was highly down‐regulated TF in CA4 under HS (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s014: In addition, other TFs exhibiting strong repression belong to TF families bHLH (4), bZIP (2), C2H2 (3), MYB (3), TCP (2), WRKY (2) and YABBY (2) (Figure 3c and Table S4). PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation.

PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s014: Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s015: The significance of post‐translational regulation in HS response is also well documented in plant HSR (Agarwal et al.,; Hahn et al.,; Miller et al.,; Mizoi et al.,; Sato et al.,; Vainonen et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s016: Evidences also suggest orchestration of multi‐layered regulatory systems comprising not only TFs but also epigenetic regulators and small RNAs as important HSR players. PMC7540533.p005.s017: In Arabidopsis, methylation of histone H3 lysine 4 functions as modulator of HS memory for survival under recurring HS conditions by HSFA2‐mediated recruitment of histone methyltransferases (Lämke et al.,).

events.event_label: HSPs function in heat response protecting PSII
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s001: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world. PMC7540533.p006.s002: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s005: Tomato HSFA2 is highly up‐regulated during HS that in turn forms a hetero‐oligomeric transcriptional complex with HSFA1. PMC7540533.p006.s006: In addition, HSP17.4‐CII acts as a co‐repressor of HSFA2 (Port et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s007: Overexpression of Arabidopsis receptor‐like kinase ERECTA (ER) leads to enhanced HS tolerance in Arabidopsis, rice and tomato with increased biomass (Shen et al.,).

events.event_label: ASAT uses acyl CoA to produce Acylsugars
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s016: Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h).

PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). events.event_label: ASAT silencing reduces WUEi and increases stomatal conductance and transpiration under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

events.event_label: Notabilis regulates thermotolerance via ABA, ROS and cell‑death pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. PMC7540533.p029.s011: However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p001.s001: Characterization of novel regulators for heat stress tolerance in tomato from Indian sub‐continent

events.event_label: HS associates_with thermo‐sensitivity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p003.s001: The footprint of tomato cultivation, a cool region crop that exhibits heat stress (HS) sensitivity, is increasing in the tropics/sub‐tropics. PMC7540533.p003.s002: Knowledge of novel regulatory hot spots from varieties growing in the Indian sub‐continent climatic zones could be vital for developing HS‐resilient crops. PMC7540533.p003.s003: Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation. PMC7540533.p003.s004: The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses.

events.event_label: ERF.C1 confers_tolerance_to HS

PMC7540533.p003.s007: Moreover, overexpression of three ethylene response transcription factors (ERF.C1/F4/F5) also improved HS tolerance in tomato. PMC7540533.p003.s008: This study identifies several novel HS tolerance genes and provides proof of their utility in tomato thermotolerance.

events.event_label: HS reduces_or_impairs yield
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s001: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world. PMC7540533.p006.s002: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,).

events.event_label: HSP17.4‐CII represses_or_inhibits HSFA2
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s005: Tomato HSFA2 is highly up‐regulated during HS that in turn forms a hetero‐oligomeric transcriptional complex with HSFA1. PMC7540533.p006.s006: In addition, HSP17.4‐CII acts as a co‐repressor of HSFA2 (Port et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s007: Overexpression of Arabidopsis receptor‐like kinase ERECTA (ER) leads to enhanced HS tolerance in Arabidopsis, rice and tomato with increased biomass (Shen et al.,).

events.event_label: HS occurs_in Tomato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s009: Till now, the knowledge about the effect of HS on tomato leaf by exploiting the comparative transcriptomics in contrasting cultivars is not well understood. PMC7540533.p006.s010: Tomato is increasingly becoming a cash crop in regions of higher temperatures (the tropics and sub‐tropics) than the optimum 26/20 °C during the photoperiod/dark period (Srivastava et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s011: One way for developing HS‐resilient crops is to exploit the naturally evolved mechanistic signalling frameworks in cultivars exhibiting contrasting response to HS (Challinor et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s012: To uncover the HS‐responsive molecular mechanisms in tomato using naturally occurring cultivars growing in the sub‐tropical climatic conditions of the Indian sub‐continent, we first evaluated nine tolerant/sensitive tomato cultivars under HS to identify the best contrasting pair, viz. PMC7540533.p006.s013: CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive).

events.event_label: heat stress changes_abundance_of yield‐related traits
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p010.s001: Identification of heat stress‐tolerant and sensitive tomato cultivars. PMC7540533.p010.s002: (a) Nine tomato cultivars with variable heat stress tolerance were screened to select best contrasting pair to heat stress response by assessing physiological‐, biochemical‐ and yield‐related traits. PMC7540533.p010.s003: Based on the performance of each cultivar, the percentage value of each trait was calculated under heat stress relative to respective controls which was set as 100%. PMC7540533.p010.s004: The heat map generated using the above data for all the cultivars divided them into four classes I to IV.

events.event_label: HS affects leaf
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s001: We next examined only that fraction of transcriptome that was significantly modulated (FDR P‐value ≤ 0.05 and fold change ≥2 and ≤−2 for up‐ and down‐regulated genes, respectively) in leaf under HS as depicted by volcano plots (Figure S2d). PMC7540533.p014.s002: A total of 6954 differentially expressing genes (DEGs) were identified between both cultivars (Figure 2a). PMC7540533.p014.s003: This number is the highest number of DEGs so far in tomato and the first for leaf utilizing contrasting cultivars (Bita et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Keller et al.,). PMC7540533.p014.s004: A slightly higher number of HS‐responsive genes were present in the HS‐tolerant cultivar (5239 vs. 5138 in CLN and CA4, respectively); moreover, the up‐regulated events were more in tolerant cultivar [2580 (CLN) and 2496 (CA4)], while almost equal number of down‐regulated genes were present in both cultivars [2659 (CLN) and 2642 (CA4)] (Figure 2a).

events.event_label: HS affects HS response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p016.s001: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response. PMC7540533.p016.s002: GO enrichment analysis suggested the involvement of up‐regulated genes in cellular response to nutrient starvation specifically phosphate starvation in addition to protein folding (Figure S3a,b). PMC7540533.p016.s003: Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c). PMC7540533.p016.s004: KEGG pathways like protein processing in ER, spliceosome and ubiquitin‐mediated proteolysis were enriched in conserved up‐regulated genes (Table S3).

PMC7540533.p016.s005: Many recent reports on stress‐responsive regulation of spliceosome per se, as well as, crucial role of alternatively spliced transcripts in response to abiotic stresses including HS have been reported in plants (Deng et al.,; Seo et al.,; Carrasco‐López et al.,; Calixto et al.,; Filichkin et al.,; Laloum et al.,; Huertas et al.,). PMC7540533.p016.s007: On the other hand, wide range of biological processes and metabolic pathways belonging to various protein classes were down‐regulated in both the cultivars (Figures S4–S6; Table S3).

events.event_label: Solyc04g077640.3.1 encodes serine carboxypeptidase
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p016.s006: The unfolded protein response (UPR) is a conserved response that is elicited by ER stress and is known to protect plants from adverse environmental stresses (Park and Park,; Wan and Jiang,). PMC7540533.p016.s007: On the other hand, wide range of biological processes and metabolic pathways belonging to various protein classes were down‐regulated in both the cultivars (Figures S4–S6; Table S3). PMC7540533.p016.s008: We found that 26 genes were highly up‐regulated (≥100‐fold) in both cultivars but none followed similar folds down‐regulation in both (Table S2). PMC7540533.p016.s009: The highly expressed genes (>1000‐fold) in CLN and CA4 belonged to the HSP protein family (Solyc03g113930.2.1, Solyc01g102960.3.1, Solyc03g082420.3.1, Solyc11g020330.1.1) and a serine carboxypeptidase (Solyc04g077640.3.1).

events.event_label: MPK1 affects thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p018.s001: In addition, some metabolic pathways were enriched specifically in up‐regulated genes in CLN only, viz. PMC7540533.p018.s002: MAPK signalling pathway, circadian rhythm, plant hormone signal transduction, sulphur metabolism and glycerolipid metabolism (Table S3). PMC7540533.p018.s003: Tomato, MPK1 regulates thermotolerance via SlSPRH1 involved in antioxidant defence (Ding et al.,). PMC7540533.p018.s004: Phosphorylation is a critical regulatory mechanism for several HSFs (Evrard et al.,; Link et al.,).

events.event_label: heat‐responsive associates_with TFs
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s001: Gene regulation by TFs acts as focal nodes in all molecular networks. PMC7540533.p020.s002: We predicted 1963 tomato TFs belonging to 58 families, using the TF prediction server of PlantTFDB (http://planttfdb.cbi.pku.edu.cn/prediction.php) with protein sequences of 35768 genes (ITAG version 3.2) as query (Table S4). PMC7540533.p020.s003: Out of these, 402 TFs belonging to 46 families were found to be heat‐responsive in at least one cultivar. PMC7540533.p020.s004: In CLN and CA4, 314 (152 down/162 up) and 285 (152 down/133 up) TFs were significantly differentially regulated, respectively (Figure 3a, Table S4).

events.event_label: Heat stress affects leaf
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p021.s001: Heat stress‐mediated regulation of transcription factors in leaf of contrasting cultivars. PMC7540533.p021.s002: (a) Venn diagram showing the common to specific heat responsiveness of transcription factors in leaf of tolerant, CLN and sensitive CA4 cultivar. PMC7540533.p021.s003: (b,c) The plots showing the highly up‐regulated [(b), FC ≥ 5; FDR P‐value ≤ 0.05] and down‐regulated [(c), FC ≤ −5; FDR P‐value ≤ 0.05] transcription factors in CLN and CA4 in response to heat stress. PMC7540533.p021.s004: (d) The comparison of cultivar‐specific heat response of various transcription factor families in leaf of tomato.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p021.s003: (b,c) The plots showing the highly up‐regulated [(b), FC ≥ 5; FDR P‐value ≤ 0.05] and down‐regulated [(c), FC ≤ −5; FDR P‐value ≤ 0.05] transcription factors in CLN and CA4 in response to heat stress. PMC7540533.p021.s004: (d) The comparison of cultivar‐specific heat response of various transcription factor families in leaf of tomato. PMC7540533.p021.s005: (e) Expression analysis of ERFs: ERF.C1, ERF.F4 and ERF.F5 in response to HS in leaf of CLN (tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive) cultivar by qRT‐PCR. PMC7540533.p021.s006: (f,g) Percentage seedling survival after HS for vector control and different p35S:ERFs overexpressing seedlings and comparative morphological analysis using hypocotyl length (g), seedling weight, shoot weight and root weight (h). PMC7540533.p021.s007: The data were calculated 6 days postheat stress imposition from four biological sets of 70 seedlings for each gene.

events.event_label: HSF family associates_with HS
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

events.event_label: HSFB1 is_detected_in tomato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated. PMC7540533.p022.s005: HSFB1 is endowed with both co‐activator as well as repressor functions in tomato, while it acts as repressor in Arabidopsis (Bharti et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s006: The Arabidopsis hsfb1‐hsfb2b double knockout mutant demonstrates higher acquired thermotolerance (Ikeda et al.,; Kumar et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s007: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4).

events.event_label: TFs enhances_or_improves thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s018: Our data on TF regulation dynamics highlight role of TF families other than the undisputed relevance of HSFs in HS response. PMC7540533.p022.s019: These TFs could be promising candidates for enhancing tomato thermotolerance as we demonstrated for the three ERFs that function as positive regulators of HS response.

events.event_label: ASAT participates_in HS response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s005: While Solyc09g014280 is induced upon potato cyst nematode infection in tomato roots (Swiecicka et al.,), Solyc05g052670 is up‐regulated in tomato in response to tomato yellow leaf curl virus infection (Chen et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s006: Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p026.s007: Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a).

events.event_label: Notabilis increases_susceptibility_to thermo‐sensitivity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p028.s001: Silencing Notabilis confers thermo‐sensitivity in tomato

events.event_label: Notabilis positively_correlates_with water stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s001: We then functionally validated the other tolerant‐up gene, Solyc07g056570 or Notabilis (9‐cis‐epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase 1). PMC7540533.p029.s002: Notabilis regulates the rate‐limiting step in the ABA biosynthesis pathway and has important role in water stress tolerance as evidenced by several reports (Burbidge et al.,; He et al.,; Luchi et al.,; Qin and Zeevaart,; Wan and Li,). PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,).

events.event_label: NCED4 associates_with thermo‐inhibition of seed germination
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s005: In lettuce, its homolog (NCED4) is the causal gene in the Htg6.1 QTL, associated with thermo‐inhibition of seed germination (Huo et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a).

events.event_label: PI contributes_to plant defence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s001: Furthermore, another gene but a ‘sensitive‐up gene’ which is Solyc03g020030 was functionally characterized. PMC7540533.p031.s002: This belongs to the proteinase inhibitor‐II (PI‐II) serine‐PI family having dimeric domains in the protein, inhibiting trypsin and chymotrypsin (Tamhane et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,).

events.event_label: PI affects leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s006: Solyc03g020030 was expressed at high levels in CLN under control condition but decreased up to threefold under HS, while significant increase was noticed in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p031.s007: We find there is enrichment of GO term of ‘serine‐type endopeptidase inhibitor activity’ in CA4‐specific up‐regulation (Figure 2c). PMC7540533.p031.s008: Silencing of PI‐II was confirmed by qRT‐PCR (Figure S11), and the leaves of knock‐down plants were upright, enduring HS better than those of vector control plants which showed drooping and burnt leaves (Figure 5a). PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c).

events.event_label: HS changes_expression_of PI
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s015: In tomato, SlbZIP1 TF (Solyc01g079480.3) transcriptionally regulates PI‐II (Zhu et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s016: When we checked our NGS data for a possible cultivar‐specific differential response of PI‐II by SlbZIP1 in response to HS, the TF was not significantly differentially regulated in CLN (1.85‐fold change) and CA4 (1.25‐fold change) (Table S2). PMC7540533.p031.s017: This suggested that HS‐mediated regulation of PI‐II is governed by other TFs or may have epigenetic regulation. PMC7540533.p031.s018: The down‐regulation of PI‐II by VIGS established its role in retrograde signalling in the regulation of HS. PMC7540533.p031.s019: Knocking down this gene by CRISPR/RNAi technology could be highly promising for engineering for HS‐thermotolerance.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: The footprint of tomato cultivation, a cool region crop that exhibits heat stress (HS) sensitivity, is increasing in the tropics/sub‐tropics.

PMC7540533.p003.s002: Knowledge of novel regulatory hot spots from varieties growing in the Indian sub‐continent climatic zones could be vital for developing HS‐resilient crops. PMC7540533.p003.s003: Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation. PMC7540533.p003.s004: The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses.

relations.subject: Acylsugar acyltransferase

evidence.text: Functional characterization of three antagonistic genes by overexpression and silencing established Solyc09g014280 (Acylsugar acyltransferase) and Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis) that are up‐regulated in tolerant cultivar, as positive regulators of HS tolerance and Solyc03g020030 (Pin‐II proteinase inhibitor), that are down‐regulated in CLN1621L, as negative regulator of thermotolerance.

relations.subject: HSFBs

evidence.text: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).

relations.evidence_preview: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s009: Till now, the knowledge about the effect of HS on tomato leaf by exploiting the comparative transcriptomics in contrasting cultivars is not well understood. PMC7540533.p006.s010: Tomato is increasingly becoming a cash crop in regions of higher temperatures (the tropics and sub‐tropics) than the optimum 26/20 °C during the photoperiod/dark period (Srivastava et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s011: One way for developing HS‐resilient crops is to exploit the naturally evolved mechanistic signalling frameworks in cultivars exhibiting contrasting response to HS (Challinor et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s012: To uncover the HS‐responsive molecular mechanisms in tomato using naturally occurring cultivars growing in the sub‐tropical climatic conditions of the Indian sub‐continent, we first evaluated nine tolerant/sensitive tomato cultivars under HS to identify the best contrasting pair, viz. PMC7540533.p006.s013: CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive).

relations.subject: heat stress

evidence.text: Based on the performance of each cultivar, the percentage value of each trait was calculated under heat stress relative to respective controls which was set as 100%.

relations.evidence_preview: Based on the performance of each cultivar, the percentage value of each trait was calculated under heat stress relative to respective controls which was set as 100%.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p010.s001: Identification of heat stress‐tolerant and sensitive tomato cultivars. PMC7540533.p010.s002: (a) Nine tomato cultivars with variable heat stress tolerance were screened to select best contrasting pair to heat stress response by assessing physiological‐, biochemical‐ and yield‐related traits. PMC7540533.p010.s003: Based on the performance of each cultivar, the percentage value of each trait was calculated under heat stress relative to respective controls which was set as 100%. PMC7540533.p010.s004: The heat map generated using the above data for all the cultivars divided them into four classes I to IV.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response.

PMC7540533.p020.s011: The plot of above 30 TFs clearly revealed that the heat‐tolerant CLN showed higher magnitude of HS‐mediated induction in majority of the TFs and common key TF pool was activated upon HS in both cultivars (Figure 3b). PMC7540533.p020.s012: Moreover, 26 TFs showed HS‐mediated strong repression (FC ≤ 5) in both cultivars (Figure 3c). relations.subject: HSF family

evidence.text: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family.

relations.evidence_preview: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

PMC7540533.p022.s011: Out of 147 ERFs reported in tomato, 30 members followed differential HS response in CLN and CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. relations.subject: Solyc09g014280

evidence.text: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).

relations.evidence_preview: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

relations.subject: ASAT gene

evidence.text: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b).

relations.evidence_preview: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

evidence.text: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance.

relations.subject: Notabilis

evidence.text: Silencing of PI‐II was confirmed by qRT‐PCR (Figure S11), and the leaves of knock‐down plants were upright, enduring HS better than those of vector control plants which showed drooping and burnt leaves (Figure 5a).

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s006: Solyc03g020030 was expressed at high levels in CLN under control condition but decreased up to threefold under HS, while significant increase was noticed in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). sentences.text: Characterization of novel regulators for heat stress tolerance in tomato from Indian sub‐continent
The footprint of tomato cultivation, a cool region crop that exhibits heat stress (HS) sensitivity, is increasing in the tropics/sub‐tropics.

Functional characterization of three antagonistic genes by overexpression and silencing established Solyc09g014280 (Acylsugar acyltransferase) and Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis) that are up‐regulated in tolerant cultivar, as positive regulators of HS tolerance and Solyc03g020030 (Pin‐II proteinase inhibitor), that are down‐regulated in CLN1621L, as negative regulator of thermotolerance.
Moreover, overexpression of three ethylene response transcription factors (ERF.

Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).

These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).
Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,).
Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,).

Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,).
The significance of post‐translational regulation in HS response is also well documented in plant HSR (Agarwal et al.,; Hahn et al.,; Miller et al.,; Mizoi et al.,; Sato et al.,; Vainonen et al.,).

Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,).

Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,).

Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,).
Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

While Solyc09g014280 is induced upon potato cyst nematode infection in tomato roots (Swiecicka et al.,), Solyc05g052670 is up‐regulated in tomato in response to tomato yellow leaf curl virus infection (Chen et al.,).
Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response.

Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a).

The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b).

Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance.

Silencing Notabilis confers thermo‐sensitivity in tomato

Notabilis regulates the rate‐limiting step in the ABA biosynthesis pathway and has important role in water stress tolerance as evidenced by several reports (Burbidge et al.,; He et al.,; Luchi et al.,; Qin and Zeevaart,; Wan and Li,).
Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,).
In lettuce, its homolog (NCED4) is the causal gene in the Htg6.
Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance.

When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a).
This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,).
Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,).

It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b).
The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance.
Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,).

Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h).
Of these HSFs, HSFA2a and HSFA7a were down‐regulated the most and appeared as the critical candidates in the Notabilis‐ABA pathway‐mediated thermotolerance.
